descriptions.description: Objective: Observer rating of pain is central to diagnosis and treatment in healthcare, especially in pediatrics. However, there are few studies examining potential biases in observer ratings of pediatric pain. Cohen et al. (2014) reported that adult participants rated a video of a child undergoing a needle stick as experiencing more pain when the child was described as a boy as compared to a girl, suggesting a possible gender bias. To confirm, clarify, and extend this finding, we conducted a replication experiment and follow-up study examining the role of explicit gender stereotypes in shaping such asymmetric judgments. Method: In an independent, pre-registered, direct replication and extension study with open data and materials (osf.io/6tj58), we showed participants the same video from Cohen et al. (2014), with the child described as a boy or a girl depending on condition. We then asked adults to rate how much pain the child experienced and displayed, how typical the child was in these respects, and how much they agreed with explicit gender stereotypes concerning pain response in boys versus girls. Results: Similar to Cohen et al. (2014), but with a larger and more demographically diverse sample, we found that the ‘boy’ was rated as experiencing more pain than the ‘girl’ despite identical clinical circumstances and identical pain behavior cross conditions. Controlling for explicit gender stereotypes eliminated the effect. Conclusions: Explicit gender stereotypes—e.g., that boys are more ‘stoic’ or girls are more emotive—may bias adult assessment of children’s pain.

titles.title: Gender Bias in Pediatric Pain Assessment

descriptions.description:
Many studies in the psychological literature have small sample sizes and are underpowered (Bakker, van Dijk, & Wicherts, 2012; Cohen, 1990; Maxwell, 2004; Stanley, Carter, & Doucouliagos, 2018), which affects generalizability, estimation of effect sizes, and accurate planning of future studies. The most effective way to increase statistical power is to increase sample size. Nevertheless, sample size is not often directly investigated. One of the studies examining sample size in psychological research over time is the study by Marszalek, Barber, Kohlhart, and Holmes (2011). In this study, data from Holmes (1979, 1983) was used. Holmes examined sample sizes in four APA journals in 1955 and 1977. These journals were Journal of Abnormal Psychology (JAbP), Journal of Applied Psychology (JApP), Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance (JEP), and Developmental Psychology (DP). According to Holmes (1979), “these journals were selected because they represent a broad coverage of psychological research” (p. 284). Marszalek et al. examined and added the sample size data from papers published in the same journals in 1995 and 2006. These years were chosen to investigate whether sample sizes increased as a result of the recommendations by Wilkinson and the Task Force on Statistical Inference (Wilkinson, 1999). They found that the sample sizes in psychology remained small and that the recommendations for increasing sample size had not been integrated in core psychological research. 

Around the time that the study by Marszalek et al. (2011) was published, the field of psychology went into a replicability crisis. Many studies failed to replicate, and the use of questionable research practices seemed to be widespread (Doyen, Klein, Pichon, & Cleeremans, 2012; John, Loewenstein, & Prelec, 2012; Open Science Collaboration, 2015). As a consequence, different initiatives were launched to improve psychological science. These initiatives include the introduction of preregistration and registered reports (Chambers, 2013; Nosek, Ebersole, DeHaven, & Mellor, 2018), the awarding of open science badges to studies that share data, materials, and/or are preregistered (Eich, 2014; Kidwell et al., 2016), and an increased focus on direct replication studies and studies with null results (Baxter & Burwell, 2017; Simons, Holcombe, & Spellman, 2014). The crisis has also increased attention to the problems of small sample sizes and underpowered studies and resulted in several calls to increase psychological studies' statistical power (Bakker, Hartgerink, Wicherts, & van der Maas, 2016; Button et al., 2013; Szucs & Ioannidis, 2017).

In the current study, we want to update the study by Marszalek et al. to investigate whether psychological studies' sample sizes have increased, specifically in response to the replicability crisis and the resulting focus on open science. We will add studies published in 2019 from the same journals. Because journals differ in how actively they adopted the open science practices, we will include two additional journals (Psychological Science (PS) and Journal of Experimental Social Psychology (JESP)) that have adopted open science practices more actively.

Our research questions are:
1. Have sample sizes increased over time?
2. Have sample sizes in psychological research increased as a reaction to the replicability crisis?
3. Does the amount of increase in sample sizes depend on Journal level policies (open science)?
4. Does the amount of increase in sample size depend on study level characteristics (open science)?
5. Additionally, we want to investigate how well automatic procedures can select the sample size compared to selecting sample sizes by hand.

subjects.subject: sample size; meta science; open science

titles.title: Sample sizes in psychological research over time

descriptions.description:
Ratings 
There are 25 quality criteria listed in Table 1 of the preprint (https://psyarxiv.com/6btc3). As we also include a few pre-prints in our sample of papers to be rated, criterion 0 (“published”) will not be rated. The other 24 criteria will be rated separately in terms of the extent to which a paper meets them, using the following scale: 1 = Criterion does not apply, 2 = Criterion largely does not apply, 3 = Criterion partly applies, 4 = Criterion largely applies, 5 = Criterion does apply. When the extent to which a criterion applies is unclear, this will be noted separately. 
Our raters are eight students working as research assistants. They will provide their ratings online, using a form that may be found at the end of this document. 

Analyses 
Our primary focus is on inter-rater agreement for ratings of the 24 individual criteria. Inter-rater agreement will be operationalized using ICC(1,1), because each article is to be rated by a group of three different raters. We consider an ICC(1,1) of .50 to be acceptable, thus our design should enable us to show that actual inter-rater agreement is significantly higher than that (see sample size calculation below). ICC(1,3) will be reported as well (it is quite possible that more than one rater is needed to achieve sufficient reliability). We will also determine the reliability of an aggregate quality score that is based on the (weighted and unweighted) sum of the individual criteria, as outlined in the preprint. 
We will also look at the distinctness of the individual criteria, that is, the question of whether they may be rated independent of one another. This will be investigated by correlating the averaged (across raters) ratings of all 24 criteria with one another (i.e., 267 correlation coefficients) and comparing them with ICC(1,3). Most correlations between criteria should be close to zero if the criteria are in fact distinct from one another. However, the outcome will depend on the specific composition of our paper sample in this regard, of course (specifically, how much the individual criteria vary independently across papers). 
We expect criteria 1a, 2a, 3a, 4a and 5a to be negatively correlated with one another (because a consensus document will usually cover just one area), and with all other criteria (because the papers we consider will usually either formulate a consensus or be empirical in nature. In the latter case, a paper may still refer to an existing consensus; criteria 1b, 2b, 3b, 4b and 5b). 
In a more exploratory fashion, we will investigate how dichotomizing the response scale (1 and 2 = “does not apply”, 3 = missing, 4 and 5 = “does apply”) affects inter-rater-agreement. 

Sampling
Sample Size Calculation. We assume that an ICC(1,1) of .50 is acceptable for ratings of this type. Using this threshold, an Alpha of .05, a Beta of .20, and a number of three raters for each paper, the required number of papers to be rated is 28 (https://wnarifin.github.io/ssc/ssicc.html; Walter, Eliasziw & Donner, 1998). Accounting for a possible drop-out rate of 20%, we need an overall sample of at least 35 papers. Multiple testing will not be accounted for, as we are interested in the overall pattern of outcomes: most or all of the 24 criteria should have inter-rater reliabilities of ICC(1,1) = .50 or higher. 
Sampling Strategy. Our goal in sampling papers was to obtain as much variation as possible along the 24 quality criteria. Thus, we aimed at identifying papers that met one or more of our criteria – while usually not meeting many others. Open calls for papers to be nominated as rating targets were issued using various channels (e.g., listservs of academic societies). These calls emphasized the positive qualities of papers that we were particularly interested in. The raters (see below) also helped with identifying such papers, but we made sure that no rater got to rate a paper that he or she had nominated. 
Papers co-authored by one or more of the authors of the criterion catalogue (Leising, Thielmann, Glöckner, Gärtner, Schönbrodt) were not considered but used as training cases for the raters. We also did some research of our own to identify target papers that would contribute to good variability on the criteria of interest. In addition, we also picked a small (n = 9) random sample from all papers published between 2016 and 2020 in three Journals (Journal of Individual Differences, Journal of Research in Personality, Personality and Indvidual Differences). 
Contacting Authors. Authors were contacted by us, made aware of the current project and asked for express permission to rate their paper, use the outcome for our analyses and publish the results. One author did not consent, so the paper was excluded. One author asked to exchange a paper that had been nominated with a more current one, with which we complied. Other than that, we received no objections. The overall sample comprises 37 papers (see below). 

Rating Schedule 
Each paper is to be independently rated by three raters (research assistants), who differ from one another in terms of availability (working hours). Given this constraint, we will assign papers to raters randomly as follows: We will fill an urn with three lots for each paper (111 lots altogether). Each time a rater signals that he or she is available to rate another paper, we will draw a lot from this urn and assign the respective paper to the rater. If a rater is assigned a paper that he or she had nominated for the ratings, the lot will be put back into the urn and we will draw again. If a rater is assigned a paper that he or she has already rated before, we will also put the lot back and draw again. The raters were instructed to strictly avoid any communication about their ratings. 

References
Walter, S.D., Eliasziw, M., & Donner, A. (1998). Sample size and optimal designs for reliability studies. Statistics in Medicine, 17, 101-110.

Management and Treatment of Patients With Major Depressive Disorder and Chronic Diseases: A Multidisciplinary Approach. Frontiers in Psychology, 11, 2510.

Appendix B: Rating Form

Criterion No//Content
1a Presents broad consensus regarding important research goals
1b Addresses important research goals that were outlined in consensus document
2a Presents broad consensus regarding terminology
2b Uses terminology from consensus document
3a Presents broad consensus regarding measurement practices
3b Uses measurement practices from consensus document
4a Presents broad consensus regarding data pre-processing and/or analysis
4b Uses consensus practices regarding data pre-processing and/or analysis
5a Presents broad consensus regarding state of knowledge and/or theory development
5b Builds directly on consensus document regarding state of knowledge and/or theory development
6a Includes algebraic or formal-logic formulation of theory being tested, and how it relates to measured variables
6b Includes account of how the tested formal theory relates to previous formulations of the same or related theories
7a Strictly separates explorative from confirmatory analyses, with the latter being pre-registered at the same level of specificity at which the results are later reported
7b Is a registered report
8 Includes at least one direct replication attempt (of others’ or own results), with a new sample and at least equal power as previous study
9a Includes pre-registered a priori power analysis / sample size planning based on specific and realistic expected effect size estimate
9b Has an expected type I error rate of <= .05 and type II error rate of <= .20, based on realistic effect size estimates
9c Demonstrates representativeness of participant samples(s) in regard to the population of interest
9d Demonstrates representativeness of stimuli in regard to the environmental conditions of interest
10a Data is made open in the form that the paper has a doi or at least a stable URL (such as OSF links) in an independent repository
10b Open data is accompanied by meta-data that (at least) documents all variables in the data set in a manner that enables new analyses without requiring further interactions with the people who collected the data
10c Code is made open (and well documented)
10d Materials are made open (and well documented)
10e All data, materials and code from a project are found in a single directory online

titles.title: Testing Inter-Rater Reliability in Judgments of Research Quality Criteria
Testing Inter-Rater Reliability in Judgments of Research Quality Criteria
